medical.title: Chinese American teens experience depression, anxiety at higher rates than peers
medical.summary: She has straight A's, a full schedule of Advanced Placement classes, a chair in the youth orchestra and a bedroom wallpapered with college acceptance letters. She also hasn't slept a full night in months. She lies awake at 2 a.m., convinced she is a burden to her family—and she has no idea how to tell anyone.

medical.title: Magnetic particle imaging may help tailor cell therapy delivery, mouse study suggests
medical.summary: Scientists at Johns Hopkins Medicine say they used a form of magnetic imaging to track cell therapy injections commonly used to treat certain autoimmune diseases and cancers. The findings, from a study of mice, add to a growing body of evidence that magnetic particle imaging (MPI), a new technique that allows scientists to visualize therapeutic cells as they inject them, may eventually help researchers personalize cell therapy treatments for individual patients. The study is published in Science Advances.

medical.title: HIV in South Africa: Why rolling out a new shot will miss a critical group of men
medical.summary: The first shipment of Lenacapavir, a long-acting injectable that prevents HIV with two shots a year, arrived in South Africa from the United States in early April 2026. Clinical trials showed close to 100% efficacy. The rollout, expected to begin in June 2026, prioritizes adolescent girls and young women, pregnant and breastfeeding women, transgender people, sex workers, men who have sex with men, and people who inject drugs.

medical.title: National index tracks AI health care regulations as policy patchwork grows
medical.summary: As artificial intelligence rapidly enters health care, policymakers and health systems are struggling to keep pace with the implications for how the technology should be governed to ensure patient safety, provider accountability, and overall equity.

medical.title: Children's brain tumors may spread faster when microglia build invasion-friendly scaffolding
medical.summary: Researchers at the Institute of Environmental Medicine (IMM), Karolinska Institutet, have identified a possible mechanism behind the spread of the aggressive brain tumor diffuse midline glioma. The study shows that the brain's own immune cells, microglia, may contribute to the tumor's invasive capacity by producing the protein fibronectin. The results are published in the journal Cell Death & Disease.

medical.title: Novel radiotracer images overactive adrenal glands to avoid invasive primary aldosteronism procedure
medical.summary: A new first-in-human study has validated a PET radiotracer that can effectively image overactive adrenal glands, offering an alternative to the highly invasive procedure currently utilized to diagnose primary aldosteronism. This approach, presented at the Society of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging 2026 Annual Meeting (SNMMI 2026), held May 30 to June 2 in Los Angeles, could give physicians a noninvasive tool to tailor treatment plans for primary aldosteronism patients.

medical.title: Comprehensive atlas maps dendritic cells across cancers
medical.summary: Researchers from VIB, VUB, and an international network of collaborators have created the most comprehensive single-cell atlas to date of tumor-associated dendritic cells. By integrating data from 14 mouse tumor models and 10 human cancer types, the study provides a detailed, cross-species view of how these key immune cells are organized and altered in cancer. The work, which is published in Nature Communications, can guide the development of next-generation therapies.

medical.title: PFAS leave fingerprints in your blood—researchers are beginning to read these clues
medical.summary: Virtually every living thing on Earth, from Patagonian penguins to newborn human babies, has been touched by the synthetic chemicals known as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or PFAS. In fact, you would be hard pressed to find a sample of human blood, tissue, or breast milk without detectable levels of at least one type of PFAS.

medical.title: Global rules could cut costs and speed access to GLP-1 drugs
medical.summary: FDA-approved carbon copies of brand-name drugs with expired patents—over the last 30 years, these generic drugs have saved trillions of dollars for hundreds of millions of people.

medical.title: Ibuprofen for joint pain: What you really need to know
medical.summary: Millions of people in the UK suffer from joint pain and arthritis. But with long wait times for scans, specialist appointments, physiotherapy, and joint replacement surgery, many people turn to over-the-counter medicines, such as ibuprofen, to manage their joint pain, stay active, and continue working.

medical.title: How cells clear immune signals could reshape drug design and cancer spread research
medical.summary: Our body receives and processes a vast number of signals. Chemical signals serve as guidance cues and ensure, for example, that immune cells arrive exactly where they are needed. Many vital processes such as sensory perception, immune responses, cardiovascular function and communication between neurons are regulated by a large group of proteins on the cell surface: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) pick up signals from the environment and transmit them to the cytoplasm of the cell.

medical.title: The link between HIV and chronic pain
medical.summary: Over half of the people carrying HIV experience chronic pain at some point, which is difficult to treat. In a new JNeurosci paper, Hui-Lin Pan, from The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, and colleagues used mice to explore how HIV leads to chronic pain.

medical.title: Enough sleep and moderate-to-vigorous activity may protect mental health in middle age, study says
medical.summary: Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity is more important for mental health in middle age than light physical activity, according to a new study by the University of Oulu and ODL Department of Sports and Exercise Medicine. The more time people spent each day engaged in moderate-to-vigorous physical activity relative to sedentary behavior and light activity, the fewer symptoms of depression and anxiety they experienced.

medical.title: When to rescue food and when to chuck it out, according to a nutritionist
medical.summary: Got some brown bananas on the counter, or soggy salad in the fridge? If so, you're not alone. Research shows on average, Australian households waste about 30% of the food we buy—or 2.5 million tons each year.

medical.title: Weight loss surgery boosts household independence for 20 years, data show
medical.summary: People who undergo bariatric surgery and see substantial weight loss are better able to cope with household tasks such as cleaning, laundry, and grocery shopping. The improvements are also sustained over a long time, according to a study at the University of Gothenburg.

medical.title: Talking about trauma doesn't always help: Brain scans show one reason why
medical.summary: After trauma, some people develop post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), a mental health condition that can involve intrusive nightmares, flashbacks, and physical reactions when reminded of the traumatic event, such as a racing heart or difficulty breathing.

medical.title: Baby brain myth fades as 300 new parents match non-parents on cognition
medical.summary: The largest and most comprehensive study of memory and cognition in new parents has found no evidence for "baby brain" in mums and dads. New mums often complain about having "baby brain," where memory and cognition become vague and unreliable. Now a Monash University study—the largest ever done comparing cognitive ability in new mums and dads—has unequivocally found no evidence that "baby brain" reflects underlying problems with cognition that result from becoming a parent.

medical.title: Teamwork, technology streamline feeding tube placement
medical.summary: Inova, the leading nonprofit health system in the Northern Virginia and Washington, D.C. metropolitan area, developed a novel dietitian-led program for the bedside placement of feeding tubes for critically ill patients. The goals were to reduce nursing workload and optimize patient safety. In addition, the system adopted an electromagnetic sensor-guided feeding tube (EMFT) system that allows clinicians to visualize the tube's path on a monitor for real-time positioning at the bedside.

medical.title: Major surgery may accelerate memory loss in 1 in 7 older adults
medical.summary: Going through surgery can take a significant toll on a patient's physical health and capabilities, especially if they are elderly. A recent study found that the effects extend far beyond mobility and pain management, as the operation may also lead to a significant loss of overall cognitive sharpness.

medical.title: The potential problems of living longer: Q&A
medical.summary: Many scientists are trying to determine just how much medical intervention can lengthen the human lifespan. But Daniel Promislow, senior scientist and scientific advisor at the Jean Mayer USDA Human Nutrition Research Center on Aging at Tufts University, is interested in a different question: If humans suddenly start living longer, what might the consequences be?

medical.title: New laser heat treatment could stop blindness before it starts
medical.summary: A new experimental treatment could finally offer hope for millions of people with dry age-related macular degeneration — one of the leading causes of blindness in older adults. Researchers at Aalto University discovered a way to gently heat tissue at the back of the eye using near-infrared light, triggering the cells’ natural “cleanup and repair” systems before major damage occurs.

medical.title: The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head
medical.summary: At a pay-as-you-can restaurant in Stroud, radical hospitality and good food are bringing strangers together The post The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors
medical.summary: British illustrator Quentin Blake has established a new museum dedicated solely to his craft, aiming to uplift other artists The post The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn’t worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
medical.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that eating beef every day may not be as risky for people with prediabetes as many assume. Researchers found that adults who ate 6–7 ounces of beef daily for a month showed no worsening in blood sugar control, insulin function, inflammation, or other key markers linked to type 2 diabetes when compared to people eating poultry instead.

medical.title: Scientists discover simple way to relieve arthritis pain without pills or surgery
medical.summary: A surprisingly simple walking tweak may offer new hope for millions living with knee osteoarthritis. In a year-long clinical trial, researchers found that slightly changing the angle of a person’s foot while walking reduced knee pain as effectively as common medications — and even slowed cartilage damage inside the joint.

science.title: From hybrids to 'virgin birth,' stick insects reveal stepwise loss of sex
science.summary: The evolution of sex remains one of biology's greatest puzzles. While sexual reproduction dominates across the animal kingdom, scientists still debate why it persists despite its high costs. Even more mysterious is the loss of sex in favor of asexual reproduction whereby females give birth to copies of themselves without any contribution from males.

science.title: Many more US voters support gay candidates, but only if they look and act 'straight,' study finds
science.summary: The period between 2018 and 2022, sometimes referred to as "the rainbow wave," featured an unprecedented increase in LGBTQ candidates elected to office. Pete Buttigieg's rise from mayor of South Bend, Indiana, to U.S. secretary of transportation with a 2020 bid for president in between sparked a national dialogue about whether gay candidates no longer faced an electoral penalty at the ballot box.

science.title: Magnetic field during catalyst synthesis triples ammonia yield
science.summary: Applying an external magnetic field during the synthesis of CoFe2O4 electrocatalysts triples the ammonia yield during electrocatalytic conversion. The magnetic field alters the surface states of the spinel oxide thin films, making catalytically active sites more accessible. In the journal Advanced Functional Materials, a team led by Marcel Risch at HZB and Sanjay Mathur at University of Cologne demonstrates a scalable strategy for developing next-generation electrocatalysts for efficient and sustainable chemical production.

science.title: Wounds may trigger 'aged' cells within hours, reshaping how senescence starts
science.summary: What if a process we associate with aging actually helps the body heal? A study led by Mikolaj Ogrodnik, LBI Trauma, published in Nature Cell Biology, shows that cells enter a state of senescence within minutes to hours after an injury—and that this rapid response not only plays a key role in wound healing, but also changes the paradigm of how slowly senescence was expected to arise.

science.title: Innovative local collaboration can unlock stronger environmental protection in England, study shows
science.summary: The use of digital tools and better coordination between different organizations can help the U.K. significantly optimize its first line of defense against ecological degradation, new research shows. Unified and local efforts can support a thriving environment and improve community well-being, University of Exeter experts have found. They hope their blueprint—which proposes tech-driven, community-led action—can rescue local environmental enforcement from funding cuts and jurisdictional confusion.

science.title: Study highlights key welfare needs for seal pups in rehabilitation
science.summary: A new study examining harbor and gray seal pups undergoing rehabilitation in the U.S. has identified important steps that could improve animal welfare during their recovery. Researchers from the University of Chester and Manchester Metropolitan University monitored 25 seal pups admitted to Tynemouth Seal Hospital after becoming stranded due to reasons such as malnourishment, injury, or abandonment, all common consequences of environmental and human related pressures around the U.K. and Ireland.

science.title: NASA to Conduct Low-Altitude Flights Near Houston
science.summary: Five research aircraft will support a Student Airborne Research Program (SARP) mission out of Ellington Field in Houston. Flights are expected from Wednesday, June 3 to Saturday, June 13. During the mission, select maneuvers will be conducted at low altitudes over the Houston area.  Pilots will fly remote sensing payloads in raster patterns, or parallel back-and-forth lines. The instruments flown could help […]

science.title: Thirty years at El Mirón cave uncover 40,000 years of Iberian prehistory
science.summary: For the past three decades, a team of archaeologists have been uncovering some of the field's most recent monumental discoveries, relying on gut instinct, persistent hard work, and cutting-edge methods and technologies.

science.title: Zebrafish microbiome model enhanced by simple trick
science.summary: A new advance in animal husbandry involving a popular aquarium fish should speed the pace of discovery in laboratory studies of host-microbe interactions, researchers report. The new findings by researchers from the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign are detailed in the journal mSystems.

science.title: What’s Up: June 2026 Skywatching Tips from NASA
science.summary: Venus and Jupiter meet after sunset, the Moon passes in front of Venus, summer begins, and deep-sky treasures rise into view.

science.title: Young and unemployed? Remote work, not AI, may be the problem, study finds
science.summary: The rise of remote work since the pandemic has made businesses more reluctant to hire young, inexperienced workers and is the key driver of higher unemployment rates for recent college graduates, a study released Monday has found.

science.title: Space station dust maps slash climate uncertainty over iron-rich particles
science.summary: New research from a team of scientists led by Cornell is transforming how researchers understand one of the atmosphere's most abundant and least understood constituents: mineral dust.

science.title: Researchers discover how to turn one germ's drug resistance into an Achilles' heel
science.summary: Decades of reliance on the antibiotic rifampicin have fueled the rise of drug-resistant Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb). But as the bacterium mutates to protect itself from the drug, it also creates new weak points that other therapies could exploit. Now, a new study published in Nature Microbiology shows that the most common rifampicin-resistance mutation slows bacterial RNA polymerase, creating vulnerabilities that future combination therapies may be able to target.

science.title: Tiny-armed alvarezsauroid dinosaurs might have been insect eaters, fossil scans suggest
science.summary: Dinosaurs are estimated to have roamed Earth for over 165 million years, gradually evolving over time to survive in changing environments. Among the many fascinating groups of dinosaurs known to have lived on our planet are alvarezsauroids.

science.title: Scientists develop virtual tomato training arena for agricultural robots
science.summary: Researchers at Osaka Metropolitan University have developed a method for creating realistic virtual tomato farms that automatically generate data for training agricultural AI systems. Their approach offers a way to overcome one of the most labor-intensive tasks in farming: harvesting the crops.

science.title: Smart sensors could help Canada tackle its $58-billion food waste problem
science.summary: Each year, Canada generates roughly $58 billion in avoidable food waste, much of which is from spoilage that goes undetected until it is already too late.

science.title: Mathematician solves origami donut efficiency challenge with fewest folds
science.summary: Most people wouldn't think that it would take rigorous mathematical proof to show how many folds it takes to make a donut shape out of paper. Yet, no one could quite figure it out until recently.

science.title: How drought rewires roots, cutting iron uptake across major food crops
science.summary: New research by scientists at the University of Calgary has found that plants, ranging from canola to rice to tomatoes, actively shut down their own ability to take up iron when they experience drought. It's a finding that could have implications for the nutritional value of agricultural crops.

science.title: Blue Origin's lunar lander just passed its toughest test yet
science.summary: There is a chamber at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston that is, in its own way, one of the most extraordinary rooms on Earth. Chamber A is one of the largest thermal vacuum facilities in the world, a vast steel vessel that can recreate the airless, temperature swinging brutality of space without leaving the ground. It has tested spacecraft destined for the moon, for the planets, and for the deep dark between them. Now it has tested Blue Origin's Blue Moon Mark 1 lunar lander, known as Endurance, and the vehicle has passed with flying colors.

science.title: Precise polymer 'knots' uncover hidden slack for designing ultra-tough and responsive smart materials
science.summary: From household plastic packaging to the flexible frameworks that support wearable electronics, polymer materials form the invisible backbone of modern life. At a microscopic level, polymers consist of long, ribbon-like molecular chains that are entangled into a disorganized mass resembling a bowl of cooked noodles.

science.title: Almost 20% of Australian students don't finish school—these 3 things can help them stay
science.summary: The latest data on Australian schooling shows about 81.5% of Year 10 students go on to Year 12.

science.title: Why dirty farm plastic matters: Cleaner mulch film could cut landfill waste and fossil fuel use
science.summary: Nearly a billion pounds of plastic film mulch is used in American agriculture each year, and most of it is dumped into landfills. New research from Washington State University shows that recycling could be a feasible alternative, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and keeping plastics out of the waste stream.

science.title: AI to rescue Australian wildlife research drowning in data
science.summary: The power of AI has been harnessed to rapidly clear a photography bottleneck and bring greater coordination and computing power to efforts to save Australian animals from extinction. Developed by researchers at The University of Queensland, the Wildlife Observatory of Australia (WildObs) can quickly analyze millions of images taken by hidden wildlife cameras, meaning faster, more accurate data to guide conservation work.

science.title: Feral horse numbers in Australia's Alps are on the rise again: It's time to act
science.summary: Last year, we noted early signs of recovery in Australia's high country, following the reduction of feral horse numbers.

science.title: Fifty-year protein mystery breaks open as acid-driven water loss comes into view
science.summary: Proteins systematically lose their protective hydration shell when their environment becomes more acidic. Until recently, this was just a theory. State-of-the-art imaging techniques have helped researchers at Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg (MLU) directly observe this process for the first time at the level of the individual water molecule. This has answered a question in biochemistry that had remained unanswered for 50 years.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: 😂 Credeai că Loredana pare tânără? Lui Nea Costel încă îi cer buletinul când îşi ia bere la birt!
mix_items.summary: Vuiesc tabloidele de frumuseţea fără bătrâneţe a Loredanei Groza, bomba sexi despre care toţi oamenii dotaţi cu ochi spun că „arată mai bine decât în 1935, când avea 20 de ani”! Dar artista nu e singura al cărei look proaspăt nu lasă să se citească vreo vârstă! Nea Costel, îndrăgitul drojdier al Pieţei Crângaşi, spune […] The post Credeai că Loredana pare tânără? Lui Nea Costel încă îi cer buletinul când îşi ia bere la birt! appeared first on Times New Roman .

Nu mai durează mult și se va da acordul pentru doborârea dronei rușești care a intrat vineri la primele ore pe teritoriul țării noastre și a căzut pe un bloc […] The post Azi au ajuns la MApN dosarele cu șină pentru aprobarea doborârii dronei de vineri din Galați appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Starea copilului în România: Trei din zece copii români trăiesc în sărăcie, tot mai mulți sunt expuși riscurilor date de lipsa vaccinării, rata mortalității la copiii sub un an crește… Articolul Copiii României: radiografie apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Pe 9 iunie, trupa germană GROZA revine la București pentru un concert pe scena Quantic, alături de Genune și Mauna Sol. Evenimentul aduce împreună trei proiecte din zona black / post-black metal, fiecare cu o direcție proprie.
mix_items.summary: GROZA, trupă germană de black metal din Bavaria, revine la București cu cel mai recent album, „Nadir” (2024), după peste 120 de concerte susținute în peste 20 de țări. În… Articolul Pe 9 iunie, trupa germană GROZA revine la București pentru un concert pe scena Quantic, alături de Genune și Mauna Sol. mix_items.summary: De Ziua Copilului, marele ecran devine locul unde aventurile încep, eroii prind viață, iar timpul petrecut împreună chiar contează. Pe 1 iunie, la Happy Cinema, copilăria vine cu aventuri cosmice,… Articolul Ce vezi la cinema de 1 iunie: aventuri, animații și povești pentru întreaga familie apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: De mai bine de zece ani mai multe organizații non-guvernamentale progresiste, dar și ceva partide politice pe care am face bine să nu le numim, promovează ideea dezincriminării consumului de marijuana în România, prima dată în scop medical apoi, cum s-a întâmplat și în alte părți, pentru consum propriu, în scop recreațional. Ideea a fost […] The post Studiu. Dacă s-ar legaliza marijuana am vorbi toți ca Georgescu appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: SOS Serviciul de Asistență Comunitară Constanţa Locație: MEDGIDIA, jud. Constanţa Număr de poziții: 2 Contract: full time Grija și încrederea unei familii, emoție pozitivă, zâmbete, suport și încurajare, nevoi de bază îndeplinite și vise transformate... The post Asistent Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. mix_items.title: From hybrids to 'virgin birth,' stick insects reveal stepwise loss of sex
mix_items.summary: The evolution of sex remains one of biology's greatest puzzles. While sexual reproduction dominates across the animal kingdom, scientists still debate why it persists despite its high costs. Even more mysterious is the loss of sex in favor of asexual reproduction whereby females give birth to copies of themselves without any contribution from males.

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Many more US voters support gay candidates, but only if they look and act 'straight,' study finds
mix_items.summary: The period between 2018 and 2022, sometimes referred to as "the rainbow wave," featured an unprecedented increase in LGBTQ candidates elected to office. Pete Buttigieg's rise from mayor of South Bend, Indiana, to U.S. secretary of transportation with a 2020 bid for president in between sparked a national dialogue about whether gay candidates no longer faced an electoral penalty at the ballot box.

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Magnetic field during catalyst synthesis triples ammonia yield
mix_items.summary: Applying an external magnetic field during the synthesis of CoFe2O4 electrocatalysts triples the ammonia yield during electrocatalytic conversion. The magnetic field alters the surface states of the spinel oxide thin films, making catalytically active sites more accessible. In the journal Advanced Functional Materials, a team led by Marcel Risch at HZB and Sanjay Mathur at University of Cologne demonstrates a scalable strategy for developing next-generation electrocatalysts for efficient and sustainable chemical production.

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Chinese American teens experience depression, anxiety at higher rates than peers
mix_items.summary: She has straight A's, a full schedule of Advanced Placement classes, a chair in the youth orchestra and a bedroom wallpapered with college acceptance letters. She also hasn't slept a full night in months. She lies awake at 2 a.m., convinced she is a burden to her family—and she has no idea how to tell anyone.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Magnetic particle imaging may help tailor cell therapy delivery, mouse study suggests
mix_items.summary: Scientists at Johns Hopkins Medicine say they used a form of magnetic imaging to track cell therapy injections commonly used to treat certain autoimmune diseases and cancers. The findings, from a study of mice, add to a growing body of evidence that magnetic particle imaging (MPI), a new technique that allows scientists to visualize therapeutic cells as they inject them, may eventually help researchers personalize cell therapy treatments for individual patients. The study is published in Science Advances.

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Wounds may trigger 'aged' cells within hours, reshaping how senescence starts
mix_items.summary: What if a process we associate with aging actually helps the body heal? A study led by Mikolaj Ogrodnik, LBI Trauma, published in Nature Cell Biology, shows that cells enter a state of senescence within minutes to hours after an injury—and that this rapid response not only plays a key role in wound healing, but also changes the paradigm of how slowly senescence was expected to arise.

mix_items.title: HIV in South Africa: Why rolling out a new shot will miss a critical group of men
mix_items.summary: The first shipment of Lenacapavir, a long-acting injectable that prevents HIV with two shots a year, arrived in South Africa from the United States in early April 2026. Clinical trials showed close to 100% efficacy. The rollout, expected to begin in June 2026, prioritizes adolescent girls and young women, pregnant and breastfeeding women, transgender people, sex workers, men who have sex with men, and people who inject drugs.

mix_items.title: National index tracks AI health care regulations as policy patchwork grows
mix_items.summary: As artificial intelligence rapidly enters health care, policymakers and health systems are struggling to keep pace with the implications for how the technology should be governed to ensure patient safety, provider accountability, and overall equity.

mix_items.title: Innovative local collaboration can unlock stronger environmental protection in England, study shows
mix_items.summary: The use of digital tools and better coordination between different organizations can help the U.K. significantly optimize its first line of defense against ecological degradation, new research shows. Unified and local efforts can support a thriving environment and improve community well-being, University of Exeter experts have found. They hope their blueprint—which proposes tech-driven, community-led action—can rescue local environmental enforcement from funding cuts and jurisdictional confusion.

mix_items.title: Children's brain tumors may spread faster when microglia build invasion-friendly scaffolding
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the Institute of Environmental Medicine (IMM), Karolinska Institutet, have identified a possible mechanism behind the spread of the aggressive brain tumor diffuse midline glioma. The study shows that the brain's own immune cells, microglia, may contribute to the tumor's invasive capacity by producing the protein fibronectin. The results are published in the journal Cell Death & Disease.

mix_items.title: Study highlights key welfare needs for seal pups in rehabilitation
mix_items.summary: A new study examining harbor and gray seal pups undergoing rehabilitation in the U.S. has identified important steps that could improve animal welfare during their recovery. Researchers from the University of Chester and Manchester Metropolitan University monitored 25 seal pups admitted to Tynemouth Seal Hospital after becoming stranded due to reasons such as malnourishment, injury, or abandonment, all common consequences of environmental and human related pressures around the U.K. and Ireland.

mix_items.title: NASA to Conduct Low-Altitude Flights Near Houston
mix_items.summary: Five research aircraft will support a Student Airborne Research Program (SARP) mission out of Ellington Field in Houston. Flights are expected from Wednesday, June 3 to Saturday, June 13. During the mission, select maneuvers will be conducted at low altitudes over the Houston area.  Pilots will fly remote sensing payloads in raster patterns, or parallel back-and-forth lines. The instruments flown could help […]

mix_items.title: Thirty years at El Mirón cave uncover 40,000 years of Iberian prehistory
mix_items.summary: For the past three decades, a team of archaeologists have been uncovering some of the field's most recent monumental discoveries, relying on gut instinct, persistent hard work, and cutting-edge methods and technologies.

mix_items.title: Novel radiotracer images overactive adrenal glands to avoid invasive primary aldosteronism procedure
mix_items.summary: A new first-in-human study has validated a PET radiotracer that can effectively image overactive adrenal glands, offering an alternative to the highly invasive procedure currently utilized to diagnose primary aldosteronism. This approach, presented at the Society of Nuclear Medicine and Molecular Imaging 2026 Annual Meeting (SNMMI 2026), held May 30 to June 2 in Los Angeles, could give physicians a noninvasive tool to tailor treatment plans for primary aldosteronism patients.

mix_items.title: Comprehensive atlas maps dendritic cells across cancers
mix_items.summary: Researchers from VIB, VUB, and an international network of collaborators have created the most comprehensive single-cell atlas to date of tumor-associated dendritic cells. By integrating data from 14 mouse tumor models and 10 human cancer types, the study provides a detailed, cross-species view of how these key immune cells are organized and altered in cancer. The work, which is published in Nature Communications, can guide the development of next-generation therapies.

mix_items.title: Zebrafish microbiome model enhanced by simple trick
mix_items.summary: A new advance in animal husbandry involving a popular aquarium fish should speed the pace of discovery in laboratory studies of host-microbe interactions, researchers report. The new findings by researchers from the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign are detailed in the journal mSystems.

mix_items.title: What’s Up: June 2026 Skywatching Tips from NASA
mix_items.summary: Venus and Jupiter meet after sunset, the Moon passes in front of Venus, summer begins, and deep-sky treasures rise into view.

mix_items.title: Young and unemployed? Remote work, not AI, may be the problem, study finds
mix_items.summary: The rise of remote work since the pandemic has made businesses more reluctant to hire young, inexperienced workers and is the key driver of higher unemployment rates for recent college graduates, a study released Monday has found.

mix_items.title: Space station dust maps slash climate uncertainty over iron-rich particles
mix_items.summary: New research from a team of scientists led by Cornell is transforming how researchers understand one of the atmosphere's most abundant and least understood constituents: mineral dust.

mix_items.title: PFAS leave fingerprints in your blood—researchers are beginning to read these clues
mix_items.summary: Virtually every living thing on Earth, from Patagonian penguins to newborn human babies, has been touched by the synthetic chemicals known as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or PFAS. In fact, you would be hard pressed to find a sample of human blood, tissue, or breast milk without detectable levels of at least one type of PFAS.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover how to turn one germ's drug resistance into an Achilles' heel
mix_items.summary: Decades of reliance on the antibiotic rifampicin have fueled the rise of drug-resistant Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb). But as the bacterium mutates to protect itself from the drug, it also creates new weak points that other therapies could exploit. Now, a new study published in Nature Microbiology shows that the most common rifampicin-resistance mutation slows bacterial RNA polymerase, creating vulnerabilities that future combination therapies may be able to target.

mix_items.title: Tiny-armed alvarezsauroid dinosaurs might have been insect eaters, fossil scans suggest
mix_items.summary: Dinosaurs are estimated to have roamed Earth for over 165 million years, gradually evolving over time to survive in changing environments. Among the many fascinating groups of dinosaurs known to have lived on our planet are alvarezsauroids.

mix_items.title: Global rules could cut costs and speed access to GLP-1 drugs
mix_items.summary: FDA-approved carbon copies of brand-name drugs with expired patents—over the last 30 years, these generic drugs have saved trillions of dollars for hundreds of millions of people.

mix_items.title: Scientists develop virtual tomato training arena for agricultural robots
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Osaka Metropolitan University have developed a method for creating realistic virtual tomato farms that automatically generate data for training agricultural AI systems. Their approach offers a way to overcome one of the most labor-intensive tasks in farming: harvesting the crops.

mix_items.title: Ibuprofen for joint pain: What you really need to know
mix_items.summary: Millions of people in the UK suffer from joint pain and arthritis. But with long wait times for scans, specialist appointments, physiotherapy, and joint replacement surgery, many people turn to over-the-counter medicines, such as ibuprofen, to manage their joint pain, stay active, and continue working.

mix_items.title: Smart sensors could help Canada tackle its $58-billion food waste problem
mix_items.summary: Each year, Canada generates roughly $58 billion in avoidable food waste, much of which is from spoilage that goes undetected until it is already too late.

mix_items.title: Mathematician solves origami donut efficiency challenge with fewest folds
mix_items.summary: Most people wouldn't think that it would take rigorous mathematical proof to show how many folds it takes to make a donut shape out of paper. Yet, no one could quite figure it out until recently.

mix_items.title: How cells clear immune signals could reshape drug design and cancer spread research
mix_items.summary: Our body receives and processes a vast number of signals. Chemical signals serve as guidance cues and ensure, for example, that immune cells arrive exactly where they are needed. Many vital processes such as sensory perception, immune responses, cardiovascular function and communication between neurons are regulated by a large group of proteins on the cell surface: G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) pick up signals from the environment and transmit them to the cytoplasm of the cell.

mix_items.title: The link between HIV and chronic pain
mix_items.summary: Over half of the people carrying HIV experience chronic pain at some point, which is difficult to treat. In a new JNeurosci paper, Hui-Lin Pan, from The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, and colleagues used mice to explore how HIV leads to chronic pain.

mix_items.title: How drought rewires roots, cutting iron uptake across major food crops
mix_items.summary: New research by scientists at the University of Calgary has found that plants, ranging from canola to rice to tomatoes, actively shut down their own ability to take up iron when they experience drought. It's a finding that could have implications for the nutritional value of agricultural crops.

mix_items.title: Blue Origin's lunar lander just passed its toughest test yet
mix_items.summary: There is a chamber at NASA's Johnson Space Center in Houston that is, in its own way, one of the most extraordinary rooms on Earth. Chamber A is one of the largest thermal vacuum facilities in the world, a vast steel vessel that can recreate the airless, temperature swinging brutality of space without leaving the ground. It has tested spacecraft destined for the moon, for the planets, and for the deep dark between them. Now it has tested Blue Origin's Blue Moon Mark 1 lunar lander, known as Endurance, and the vehicle has passed with flying colors.

mix_items.title: Enough sleep and moderate-to-vigorous activity may protect mental health in middle age, study says
mix_items.summary: Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity is more important for mental health in middle age than light physical activity, according to a new study by the University of Oulu and ODL Department of Sports and Exercise Medicine. The more time people spent each day engaged in moderate-to-vigorous physical activity relative to sedentary behavior and light activity, the fewer symptoms of depression and anxiety they experienced.

mix_items.title: When to rescue food and when to chuck it out, according to a nutritionist
mix_items.summary: Got some brown bananas on the counter, or soggy salad in the fridge? If so, you're not alone. Research shows on average, Australian households waste about 30% of the food we buy—or 2.5 million tons each year.

mix_items.title: Precise polymer 'knots' uncover hidden slack for designing ultra-tough and responsive smart materials
mix_items.summary: From household plastic packaging to the flexible frameworks that support wearable electronics, polymer materials form the invisible backbone of modern life. At a microscopic level, polymers consist of long, ribbon-like molecular chains that are entangled into a disorganized mass resembling a bowl of cooked noodles.

mix_items.title: Almost 20% of Australian students don't finish school—these 3 things can help them stay
mix_items.summary: The latest data on Australian schooling shows about 81.5% of Year 10 students go on to Year 12.

mix_items.title: Why dirty farm plastic matters: Cleaner mulch film could cut landfill waste and fossil fuel use
mix_items.summary: Nearly a billion pounds of plastic film mulch is used in American agriculture each year, and most of it is dumped into landfills. New research from Washington State University shows that recycling could be a feasible alternative, reducing reliance on fossil fuels and keeping plastics out of the waste stream.

mix_items.title: Weight loss surgery boosts household independence for 20 years, data show
mix_items.summary: People who undergo bariatric surgery and see substantial weight loss are better able to cope with household tasks such as cleaning, laundry, and grocery shopping. The improvements are also sustained over a long time, according to a study at the University of Gothenburg.

mix_items.title: AI to rescue Australian wildlife research drowning in data
mix_items.summary: The power of AI has been harnessed to rapidly clear a photography bottleneck and bring greater coordination and computing power to efforts to save Australian animals from extinction. Developed by researchers at The University of Queensland, the Wildlife Observatory of Australia (WildObs) can quickly analyze millions of images taken by hidden wildlife cameras, meaning faster, more accurate data to guide conservation work.

mix_items.title: Talking about trauma doesn't always help: Brain scans show one reason why
mix_items.summary: After trauma, some people develop post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), a mental health condition that can involve intrusive nightmares, flashbacks, and physical reactions when reminded of the traumatic event, such as a racing heart or difficulty breathing.

mix_items.title: Baby brain myth fades as 300 new parents match non-parents on cognition
mix_items.summary: The largest and most comprehensive study of memory and cognition in new parents has found no evidence for "baby brain" in mums and dads. New mums often complain about having "baby brain," where memory and cognition become vague and unreliable. Now a Monash University study—the largest ever done comparing cognitive ability in new mums and dads—has unequivocally found no evidence that "baby brain" reflects underlying problems with cognition that result from becoming a parent.

mix_items.title: Feral horse numbers in Australia's Alps are on the rise again: It's time to act
mix_items.summary: Last year, we noted early signs of recovery in Australia's high country, following the reduction of feral horse numbers.

mix_items.title: Fifty-year protein mystery breaks open as acid-driven water loss comes into view
mix_items.summary: Proteins systematically lose their protective hydration shell when their environment becomes more acidic. Until recently, this was just a theory. State-of-the-art imaging techniques have helped researchers at Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg (MLU) directly observe this process for the first time at the level of the individual water molecule. This has answered a question in biochemistry that had remained unanswered for 50 years.

mix_items.title: Teamwork, technology streamline feeding tube placement
mix_items.summary: Inova, the leading nonprofit health system in the Northern Virginia and Washington, D.C. metropolitan area, developed a novel dietitian-led program for the bedside placement of feeding tubes for critically ill patients. The goals were to reduce nursing workload and optimize patient safety. In addition, the system adopted an electromagnetic sensor-guided feeding tube (EMFT) system that allows clinicians to visualize the tube's path on a monitor for real-time positioning at the bedside.

mix_items.title: Major surgery may accelerate memory loss in 1 in 7 older adults
mix_items.summary: Going through surgery can take a significant toll on a patient's physical health and capabilities, especially if they are elderly. A recent study found that the effects extend far beyond mobility and pain management, as the operation may also lead to a significant loss of overall cognitive sharpness.

mix_items.title: The potential problems of living longer: Q&A
mix_items.summary: Many scientists are trying to determine just how much medical intervention can lengthen the human lifespan. But Daniel Promislow, senior scientist and scientific advisor at the Jean Mayer USDA Human Nutrition Research Center on Aging at Tufts University, is interested in a different question: If humans suddenly start living longer, what might the consequences be?

mix_items.title: New laser heat treatment could stop blindness before it starts
mix_items.summary: A new experimental treatment could finally offer hope for millions of people with dry age-related macular degeneration — one of the leading causes of blindness in older adults. Researchers at Aalto University discovered a way to gently heat tissue at the back of the eye using near-infrared light, triggering the cells’ natural “cleanup and repair” systems before major damage occurs.

mix_items.title: The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head
mix_items.summary: At a pay-as-you-can restaurant in Stroud, radical hospitality and good food are bringing strangers together The post The English restaurant turning hospitality on its head appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors
mix_items.summary: British illustrator Quentin Blake has established a new museum dedicated solely to his craft, aiming to uplift other artists The post The world’s largest museum of illustration is about to open its doors appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Surprising study finds beef doesn’t worsen blood sugar or diabetes risk
mix_items.summary: A new clinical trial suggests that eating beef every day may not be as risky for people with prediabetes as many assume. Researchers found that adults who ate 6–7 ounces of beef daily for a month showed no worsening in blood sugar control, insulin function, inflammation, or other key markers linked to type 2 diabetes when compared to people eating poultry instead.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover simple way to relieve arthritis pain without pills or surgery
mix_items.summary: A surprisingly simple walking tweak may offer new hope for millions living with knee osteoarthritis. In a year-long clinical trial, researchers found that slightly changing the angle of a person’s foot while walking reduced knee pain as effectively as common medications — and even slowed cartilage damage inside the joint.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Am zis ca fac sport. Am facut: am fugit de responsabilitati.

satire_ro.title: Localnici din Sintești detașați la Galați au prins o dronă rusească din zbor și au topit-o pentru metale
